title: Der Räuberbräutigam
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 40. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The Robber Bridegroom (KHM 40), one of the darkest tales in the collection: a miller betroths his daughter to a rich suitor whose house lies deep in the wood, where she discovers a den of cannibal robbers and, hidden behind a barrel, witnesses them murder and dismember another maiden — a severed ring-finger falling into her lap becomes the proof that damns them. At the wedding feast she tells the whole horror aloud as a mere dream, soothing the bridegroom with “that was only a dream, my darling” until she produces the finger. Spelling follows the 1857 seventh edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, Faß, Schooß, Thüre, ward, gieng/giengen, hieng, rothen, tödten, verheirathet, hingerathen and Schandthaten.

sentences.t: Es war einmal ein Müller, der hatte eine schöne Tochter, und als sie herangewachsen war, so wünschte er sie wäre versorgt und gut verheirathet: er dachte „kommt ein ordentlicher Freier und hält um sie an, so will ich sie ihm geben.“
sentences.l: There was once a miller, who had a beautiful daughter, and when she had grown up, he wished she were provided for and well married: he thought, “if a proper suitor comes and asks for her, then I will give her to him.”
sentences.i: Once upon a time there was a miller who had a beautiful daughter, and when she had grown up, he wished to see her provided for and well married; he thought, “If a respectable suitor comes and asks for her hand, I will give her to him.”
sentences.n: “wäre ... versorgt” is subjunctive II of his wish. “hält ... an” is the separable verb anhalten (um jemanden anhalten = to ask for someone’s hand); the prefix an- jumps to the end. “Es war einmal” is the fixed fairy-tale opening.

sentences.t: Nicht lange so kam ein Freier, der schien sehr reich zu sein, und da der Müller nichts an ihm auszusetzen wußte, so versprach er ihm seine Tochter.
sentences.l: Not long, and there came a suitor, who seemed to be very rich, and since the miller knew nothing to find fault with in him, he promised him his daughter.
sentences.i: Before long a suitor came who seemed to be very rich, and since the miller could find nothing to object to in him, he promised him his daughter.
sentences.n: “etwas an jemandem auszusetzen haben/wissen” = to find fault with someone. “wußte” is the 1857 spelling of wusste.

sentences.t: Das Mädchen aber hatte ihn nicht so recht lieb, wie eine Braut ihren Bräutigam lieb haben soll, und hatte kein Vertrauen zu ihm: so oft sie ihn ansah oder an ihn dachte, fühlte sie ein Grauen in ihrem Herzen.
sentences.l: The girl, however, did not have him rightly dear, as a bride ought to hold her bridegroom dear, and had no trust in him: as often as she looked at him or thought of him, she felt a dread in her heart.
sentences.i: But the girl did not truly love him, as a bride should love her bridegroom, and she had no trust in him: whenever she looked at him or thought of him, she felt a dread in her heart.
sentences.n: “lieb haben” = to love, hold dear. “sah ... an” is the separable verb ansehen (to look at). “ein Grauen” = a shudder, a feeling of dread.

sentences.t: Einmal sprach er zu ihr „du bist meine Braut und besuchst mich nicht einmal.“
sentences.l: Once he spoke to her, “you are my bride and do not even visit me.”
sentences.i: One day he said to her, “You are my bride, and you never even come to visit me.”
sentences.n: “nicht einmal” = not even. “besuchst” is 2nd person singular of besuchen (to visit).

sentences.t: Das Mädchen antwortete „ich weiß nicht wo euer Haus ist.“
sentences.l: The girl answered, “I do not know where your house is.”
sentences.n: “euer” is the possessive of the polite/plural ihr — ‘your.’

sentences.t: Da sprach der Bräutigam „mein Haus ist draußen im dunkeln Wald.“
sentences.l: Then the bridegroom spoke, “my house is out in the dark wood.”
sentences.n: “dunkeln” is the inflected dative of dunkel (dark); “im” = in dem.

sentences.t: Es suchte Ausreden und meinte es könnte den Weg dahin nicht finden.
sentences.l: It sought excuses and thought it could not find the way there.
sentences.i: She sought excuses and said she could not find the way there.
sentences.n: “Es” refers to das Mädchen (neuter), so grammatically ‘it’ — read it as ‘she.’ “könnte” is subjunctive of reported speech (‘that she could not’).

sentences.t: Der Bräutigam sagte „künftigen Sonntag muß du hinaus zu mir kommen, ich habe die Gäste schon eingeladen, und damit du den Weg durch den Wald findest, so will ich dir Asche streuen.“
sentences.l: The bridegroom said, “next Sunday you must come out to me; I have already invited the guests, and so that you find the way through the wood, I will strew ashes for you.”
sentences.i: The bridegroom said, “Next Sunday you must come out to me; I have already invited the guests, and so that you may find the way through the wood, I will strew ashes for you.”
sentences.n: “muß du” for modern musst du (the 1857 text drops the -t before du). “damit” = so that (introduces a clause of purpose). “streuen” = to strew, scatter.

sentences.t: Als der Sonntag kam und das Mädchen sich auf den Weg machen sollte, ward ihm so angst, es wußte selbst nicht recht warum, und damit es den Weg bezeichnen könnte, steckte es sich beide Taschen voll Erbsen und Linsen.
sentences.l: When the Sunday came and the girl was to set out on her way, it became so afraid to it — it did not itself rightly know why — and so that it could mark the way, it stuffed both its pockets full of peas and lentils.
sentences.i: When Sunday came and the girl was to set out, she grew so frightened — she did not rightly know why herself — and so that she could mark the way, she stuffed both her pockets full of peas and lentils.
sentences.n: “ward ihm so angst” is an impersonal dative idiom — ‘she became so afraid’ (literally ‘it grew so anxious to her’); “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden. “bezeichnen” = to mark, denote.

sentences.t: An dem Eingang des Waldes war Asche gestreut, der ging es nach, warf aber bei jedem Schritt rechts und links ein paar Erbsen auf die Erde.
sentences.l: At the entrance of the wood ashes were strewn; it followed after them, but threw at every step, right and left, a few peas onto the ground.
sentences.i: At the entrance of the wood ashes had been strewn; she followed them, but at every step threw a few peas to right and left onto the ground.
sentences.n: “der ging es nach” — nachgehen (to follow) takes the dative; der refers back to the ashes. “Eingang” = entrance.

sentences.t: Es gieng fast den ganzen Tag bis es mitten in den Wald kam, wo er am dunkelsten war, da stand ein einsames Haus, das gefiel ihm nicht, denn es sah so finster und unheimlich aus.
sentences.l: It went almost the whole day until it came into the middle of the wood, where it was at its darkest; there stood a solitary house, which did not please it, for it looked so gloomy and uncanny.
sentences.i: She walked almost the whole day until she came to the middle of the wood, where it was darkest; there stood a solitary house, which she did not like, for it looked so gloomy and eerie.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging. “am dunkelsten” = at its darkest (superlative of dunkel). “sah ... aus” is the separable verb aussehen (to look, appear); finster = gloomy, unheimlich = uncanny, eerie.

sentences.t: Es trat hinein, aber es war niemand darin und herrschte die größte Stille.
sentences.l: It stepped in, but there was no one within, and the greatest silence reigned.
sentences.i: She stepped inside, but there was no one within, and the deepest silence reigned.
sentences.n: “trat hinein” — hineintreten (to step in); hinein marks motion inward, away from the speaker.

sentences.t: Plötzlich rief eine Stimme
sentences.l: Suddenly a voice cried
sentences.i: Suddenly a voice called out:

sentences.t:
„kehr um, kehr um, du junge Braut,
du bist in einem Mörderhaus.“
sentences.l:
“turn back, turn back, you young bride,
you are in a murderers’ house.”
sentences.n: “kehr um” is the imperative of umkehren (to turn back), the separable prefix um coming first in the command. This warning refrain recurs three times, and the bride will repeat it word for word in her dream-telling at the end. Mörderhaus = murderers’ house.

sentences.t: Das Mädchen blickte auf und sah daß die Stimme von einem Vogel kam, der da in einem Bauer an der Wand hieng.
sentences.l: The girl looked up and saw that the voice came from a bird that hung there in a cage on the wall.
sentences.i: The girl looked up and saw that the voice came from a bird hanging there in a cage on the wall.
sentences.n: “blickte auf” is separable (aufblicken, to look up). “Bauer” here is the neuter/masculine word for a bird-cage, not a farmer. “hieng” is the 1857 spelling of hing (past of hängen). “daß” is the 1857 spelling of dass.

sentences.t: Nochmals rief er
sentences.l: Once more it cried
sentences.i: Once again it called:
sentences.n: “er” refers to der Vogel (masculine) — grammatically ‘he,’ read as ‘it.’ nochmals = once more.

sentences.t:
„kehr um, kehr um, du junge Braut,
du bist in einem Mörderhaus.“
sentences.l:
“turn back, turn back, you young bride,
you are in a murderers’ house.”

sentences.t: Da gieng die schöne Braut weiter aus einer Stube in die andere und ging durch das ganze Haus, aber es war alles leer und keine Menschenseele zu finden.
sentences.l: Then the beautiful bride went on, out of one room into the other, and went through the whole house, but all was empty and not a living soul to be found.
sentences.i: Then the beautiful bride went on from one room into the next and passed through the whole house, but everything was empty and not a living soul to be found.
sentences.n: “Menschenseele” (literally ‘human-soul’) = a living soul; “keine Menschenseele” = not a soul.

sentences.t: Endlich kam sie auch in den Keller, da saß eine steinalte Frau, die wackelte mit dem Kopfe.
sentences.l: At last she came also into the cellar; there sat a stone-old woman, who wagged with her head.
sentences.i: At last she came into the cellar too, where there sat an ancient woman, wagging her head.
sentences.n: “steinalt” (literally ‘stone-old’) = very old, ancient. “Kopfe” has the old dative -e ending. “wackelte mit dem Kopfe” = shook/wagged her head.

sentences.t: „Könnt ihr mir nicht sagen,“ sprach das Mädchen, „ob mein Bräutigam hier wohnt?“
sentences.l: “Can you not tell me,” spoke the girl, “whether my bridegroom lives here?”
sentences.i: “Can you not tell me,” said the girl, “whether my bridegroom lives here?”
sentences.n: “ihr” is the older respectful plural address to the old woman (‘you’). “ob” = whether.

sentences.t: „Ach, du armes Kind,“ antwortete die Alte, „wo bist du hingerathen!
sentences.l: “Oh, you poor child,” answered the old woman, “where have you strayed to!
sentences.i: “Oh, you poor child,” answered the old woman, “what a place you have wandered into!
sentences.n: “die Alte” = the old woman (adjective used as a noun). “hingerathen” is the 1857 spelling of hingeraten (hingeraten = to end up, stray to); the separable prefix hin- marks direction.

sentences.t: du bist in einer Mördergrube.
sentences.l: you are in a murderers’ den.
sentences.n: “Mördergrube” (literally ‘murderers’ pit’) = a den of murderers; the word survives in the idiom aus seinem Herzen keine Mördergrube machen.

sentences.t: Du meinst du wärst eine Braut, die bald Hochzeit macht, aber du wirst die Hochzeit mit dem Tode halten.
sentences.l: You think you were a bride who soon holds a wedding, but you will hold the wedding with death.
sentences.i: You think you are a bride who will soon hold her wedding, but you will keep your wedding with Death.
sentences.n: “wärst” is subjunctive II of sein in the reported thought (‘that you were / are’). “Tode” has the old dative -e. Grim wordplay: Hochzeit halten (to hold a wedding) turned into a wedding with death.

sentences.t: Siehst du, da hab ich einen großen Kessel mit Wasser aufsetzen müssen, wenn sie dich in ihrer Gewalt haben, so zerhacken sie dich ohne Barmherzigkeit, kochen dich und essen dich, denn es sind Menschenfresser.
sentences.l: You see, there I have had to set on a great kettle with water; when they have you in their power, then they hack you to pieces without mercy, cook you and eat you, for they are man-eaters.
sentences.i: You see, I have had to set a great kettle of water on the fire; once they have you in their power, they will hack you to pieces without mercy, cook you and eat you, for they are cannibals.
sentences.n: “aufsetzen” (to set on, put on to boil) is separable. “zerhacken” = to hack to pieces (zer- means apart, to bits). Barmherzigkeit = mercy; Menschenfresser (literally ‘people-eaters’) = cannibals.

sentences.t: Wenn ich nicht Mitleiden mit dir habe und dich rette, so bist du verloren.“
sentences.l: If I have no compassion with you and do not save you, then you are lost.”
sentences.i: If I do not take pity on you and save you, then you are lost.”
sentences.n: “Mitleiden mit jemandem haben” = to have compassion for someone. “rette” = 1st sg. of retten (to save). verloren = lost (past participle of verlieren).

sentences.t: Darauf führte es die Alte hinter ein großes Faß, wo man es nicht sehen konnte.
sentences.l: Thereupon the old woman led it behind a great barrel, where one could not see it.
sentences.i: Then the old woman led her behind a great barrel, where she could not be seen.
sentences.n: Word order: “führte es die Alte” — es (the girl, object) stands first for emphasis, die Alte is the subject. “Faß” is the 1857 spelling of Fass (barrel, cask).

sentences.t: „Sei wie ein Mäuschen still,“ sagte sie, „rege dich nicht und bewege dich nicht, sonst ists um dich geschehen.
sentences.l: “Be still as a little mouse,” she said, “do not stir and do not move, otherwise it is over with you.
sentences.i: “Be as quiet as a little mouse,” she said, “do not stir and do not move, or else it is all over with you.
sentences.n: “Mäuschen” is the diminutive of Maus (little mouse). “rege dich” (sich regen) and “bewege dich” (sich bewegen) both = to stir, move. “ists um dich geschehen” — the idiom es ist um jemanden geschehen = ‘it is all over with someone.’ ists = ist es.

sentences.t: Nachts wenn die Räuber schlafen, wollen wir entfliehen, ich habe schon lange auf eine Gelegenheit gewartet.“
sentences.l: At night when the robbers sleep, we will flee; I have long waited for an opportunity.”
sentences.i: Tonight, when the robbers are asleep, we will flee; I have long been waiting for an opportunity.”
sentences.n: “entfliehen” = to flee, escape. “auf eine Gelegenheit warten” = to wait for an opportunity.

sentences.t: Kaum war das geschehen, so kam die gottlose Rotte nach Haus.
sentences.l: Scarcely had that happened, when the godless pack came home.
sentences.i: Scarcely had this been done when the godless band came home.
sentences.n: “Kaum ... so ...” = ‘scarcely ... when ...’ “Rotte” = a rabble, gang, pack. gottlos = godless, wicked.

sentences.t: Sie brachten eine andere Jungfrau mitgeschleppt, waren trunken und hörten nicht auf ihr Schreien und Jammern.
sentences.l: They brought another maiden dragged along; they were drunk and did not heed her screaming and wailing.
sentences.i: They came dragging another maiden along; they were drunk and paid no heed to her screaming and wailing.
sentences.n: “mitgeschleppt” is the past participle of mitschleppen (to drag along; separable mit-). “hörten nicht auf” — here in the sense ‘did not heed’ (auf etwas hören = to heed). trunken = drunk (an elevated word for betrunken).

sentences.t: Sie gaben ihr Wein zu trinken, drei Gläser voll, ein Glas weißen, ein Glas rothen, und ein Glas gelben, davon zersprang ihr das Herz.
sentences.l: They gave her wine to drink, three glasses full, a glass of white, a glass of red, and a glass of yellow, from which her heart burst asunder.
sentences.i: They gave her wine to drink, three glasses full — a glass of white, a glass of red, and a glass of yellow — and from it her heart burst apart.
sentences.n: “weißen ... rothen ... gelben” are the wine-colours in the accusative (agreeing with an understood Wein). “rothen” is the 1857 spelling of roten. “zersprang” (zerspringen) = burst apart; “ihr das Herz” — the dative ihr shows whose heart.

sentences.t: Darauf rissen sie ihr die feinen Kleider ab, legten sie auf einen Tisch, zerhackten ihren schönen Leib in Stücke und streuten Salz darüber.
sentences.l: Thereupon they tore her fine clothes off, laid her on a table, hacked her beautiful body into pieces, and strewed salt over it.
sentences.i: Then they tore off her fine clothes, laid her on a table, hacked her beautiful body to pieces, and scattered salt over it.
sentences.n: “rissen ... ab” is the separable verb abreißen (to tear off); the dative ihr = ‘off her.’ “zerhackten ... in Stücke” = hacked to pieces. Nothing is softened here — the tale is deliberately grim.

sentences.t: Die arme Braut hinter dem Faß zitterte und bebte, denn sie sah wohl was für ein Schicksal ihr die Räuber zugedacht hatten.
sentences.l: The poor bride behind the barrel trembled and shook, for she saw well what a fate the robbers had intended for her.
sentences.i: The poor bride behind the barrel trembled and quaked, for she saw plainly what a fate the robbers had marked out for her.
sentences.n: “was für ein” = ‘what (sort of),’ here ‘what a.’ “zugedacht” is the past participle of zudenken (jemandem etwas zudenken = to intend/destine something for someone). sah wohl = saw well/plainly.

sentences.t: Einer von ihnen bemerkte an dem kleinen Finger der Gemordeten einen goldenen Ring, und als er sich nicht gleich abziehen ließ, so nahm er ein Beil und hackte den Finger ab: aber der Finger sprang in die Höhe über das Faß hinweg und fiel der Braut gerade in den Schooß.
sentences.l: One of them noticed on the little finger of the murdered woman a golden ring, and as it would not at once let itself be drawn off, he took a hatchet and hacked the finger off: but the finger sprang up into the air, over the barrel and away, and fell right into the bride’s lap.
sentences.i: One of them noticed a gold ring on the murdered girl’s little finger, and as it would not come off at once, he took a hatchet and hacked the finger off; but the finger sprang up into the air, flew over the barrel, and fell right into the bride’s lap.
sentences.n: “der Gemordeten” = of the murdered woman (substantivized past participle of morden, genitive). “sich abziehen ließ” — abziehen (to draw off) with the reflexive-passive ‘let itself be pulled off.’ Beil = hatchet; “hackte ... ab” is separable (abhacken). “in die Höhe” = up into the air; hinweg = away, off. Schooß is the 1857 spelling of Schoß (lap).

sentences.t: Der Räuber nahm ein Licht und wollte ihn suchen, konnte ihn aber nicht finden.
sentences.l: The robber took a light and wanted to look for it, but could not find it.
sentences.i: The robber took a light and wanted to look for it, but could not find it.
sentences.n: “ihn” refers to der Finger (masculine) — ‘it,’ the finger.

sentences.t: Da sprach ein anderer „hast du auch schon hinter dem großen Fasse gesucht?“
sentences.l: Then another spoke, “have you already looked behind the great barrel too?”
sentences.i: Then another said, “Have you looked behind the great barrel as well?”
sentences.n: “Fasse” has the old dative -e (Faß → Fasse). “auch schon” = already, as well.

sentences.t: Aber die Alte rief, „kommt und eßt, und laßt das Suchen bis Morgen: der Finger läuft euch nicht fort.“
sentences.l: But the old woman cried, “come and eat, and leave the searching until morning: the finger will not run away from you.”
sentences.i: But the old woman cried, “Come and eat, and leave the searching till morning — the finger will not run away from you.”
sentences.n: “eßt” is the 1857 spelling of esst (imperative pl. of essen). “laßt” = leave (imperative of lassen). “läuft ... fort” is the separable verb fortlaufen (to run away); the dative euch = ‘from you.’

sentences.t: Da sprachen die Räuber „die Alte hat Recht,“ ließen vom Suchen ab, setzten sich zum Essen, und die Alte tröpfelte ihnen einen Schlaftrunk in den Wein, daß sie sich bald in den Keller hinlegten, schliefen und schnarchten.
sentences.l: Then the robbers spoke, “the old woman is right,” left off the searching, sat down to the meal, and the old woman dripped a sleeping-draught into their wine, so that they soon lay down in the cellar, slept and snored.
sentences.i: Then the robbers said, “The old woman is right,” gave up the search, and sat down to eat; and the old woman trickled a sleeping-draught into their wine, so that they soon lay down in the cellar, and slept and snored.
sentences.n: “Recht haben” = to be right. “ließen ... ab” is the separable verb ablassen (von etwas ablassen = to leave off). Schlaftrunk = sleeping-draught. “hinlegten” is separable (sich hinlegen, to lie down). tröpfelte = trickled, dripped.

sentences.t: Als die Braut das hörte, kam sie hinter dem Faß hervor, und mußte über die Schlafenden wegschreiten, die da reihenweise auf der Erde lagen, und hatte große Angst sie möchte einen aufwecken.
sentences.l: When the bride heard that, she came forth from behind the barrel, and had to stride over the sleepers, who lay there in rows on the ground, and was in great fear she might wake one of them.
sentences.i: When the bride heard this, she came out from behind the barrel and had to step over the sleepers, who lay there in rows on the floor, and she was in great fear that she might wake one of them.
sentences.n: “kam ... hervor” is separable (hervorkommen, to come forth). “wegschreiten” = to stride away/over (separable weg-). reihenweise = in rows. “möchte ... aufwecken” — subjunctive of fear (‘might wake’); aufwecken (to wake up) is separable.

sentences.t: Aber Gott half ihr daß sie glücklich durchkam, die Alte stieg mit ihr hinauf, öffnete die Thüre, und sie eilten so schnell sie konnten aus der Mördergrube fort.
sentences.l: But God helped her that she got through safely; the old woman climbed up with her, opened the door, and they hastened away out of the murderers’ den as fast as they could.
sentences.i: But God helped her to get through safely; the old woman climbed up with her, opened the door, and they hurried out of the murderers’ den as fast as they could.
sentences.n: “helfen” takes the dative (half ihr = helped her). “durchkam” is separable (durchkommen, to get through). “Thüre” carries the old -th- (modern Tür). “eilten ... fort” — fort marks off/away. glücklich here = safely, luckily.

sentences.t: Die gestreute Asche hatte der Wind weggeweht, aber die Erbsen und Linsen hatten gekeimt und waren aufgegangen, und zeigten im Mondenschein den Weg.
sentences.l: The strewn ashes the wind had blown away, but the peas and lentils had sprouted and come up, and showed the way in the moonlight.
sentences.i: The wind had blown away the strewn ashes, but the peas and lentils had sprouted and come up, and showed the way in the moonlight.
sentences.n: “gestreute” = strewn (past participle of streuen used as an adjective). “weggeweht” is the past participle of wegwehen (to blow away; separable weg-). gekeimt = sprouted (keimen). “waren aufgegangen” — aufgehen here of seeds coming up. Mondenschein is an older form of Mondschein (moonlight).

sentences.t: Sie giengen die ganze Nacht bis sie Morgens in der Mühle ankamen.
sentences.l: They went the whole night until in the morning they arrived at the mill.
sentences.i: They walked the whole night until they arrived at the mill in the morning.
sentences.n: “giengen” is the 1857 spelling of gingen. “ankamen” is the preterite of ankommen (to arrive; separable an-). Morgens = in the morning.

sentences.t: Da erzählte das Mädchen seinem Vater alles wie es sich zugetragen hatte.
sentences.l: Then the girl told its father everything, how it had come to pass.
sentences.i: Then the girl told her father everything, how it had all come to pass.
sentences.n: “sich zutragen” = to come about, happen (a solemn fairy-tale phrase). “seinem Vater” — seinem because das Mädchen is grammatically neuter.

sentences.t: Als der Tag kam wo die Hochzeit sollte gehalten werden, erschien der Bräutigam, der Müller aber hatte alle seine Verwandte und Bekannte einladen lassen.
sentences.l: When the day came when the wedding was to be held, the bridegroom appeared, but the miller had had all his relatives and acquaintances invited.
sentences.i: When the day came on which the wedding was to be held, the bridegroom appeared; but the miller had had all his relatives and acquaintances invited.
sentences.n: “sollte gehalten werden” = was to be held (passive with sollen). “einladen lassen” = to have (someone) invited; einladen = to invite. Verwandte = relatives, Bekannte = acquaintances.

sentences.t: Wie sie bei Tische saßen, ward einem jeden aufgegeben etwas zu erzählen.
sentences.l: As they sat at table, it was set to each one to tell something.
sentences.i: As they sat at table, each one was called upon to tell a tale.
sentences.n: “bei Tische” = at table (Tische with the old dative -e). “ward ... aufgegeben” — the impersonal passive ‘it was assigned’ (ward is the archaic preterite of werden); jemandem etwas aufgeben = to set someone a task. “einem jeden” = to each one (dative).

sentences.t: Die Braut saß still und redete nichts.
sentences.l: The bride sat silent and said nothing.
sentences.i: The bride sat silent and said nothing.

sentences.t: Da sprach der Bräutigam zur Braut „nun, mein Herz, weißt du nichts? erzähl uns auch etwas.“
sentences.l: Then the bridegroom spoke to the bride, “now, my heart, do you know nothing? tell us something too.”
sentences.i: Then the bridegroom said to the bride, “Well, my heart, do you know nothing? Tell us something too.”
sentences.n: “mein Herz” = ‘my heart,’ a term of endearment. “erzähl” is the imperative of erzählen (tell!).

sentences.t: Sie antwortete „so will ich einen Traum erzählen.
sentences.l: She answered, “then I will tell a dream.
sentences.i: She answered, “Then I will tell a dream.
sentences.n: Here the tale’s frame device begins: the bride recounts the whole horror as if it were a dream (Traum), reassuring the murderer at each turn with “Mein Schatz, das träumte mir nur” — until the finger proves it real.

sentences.t: Ich gieng allein durch einen Wald und kam endlich zu einem Haus, da war keine Menschenseele darin, aber an der Wand war ein Vogel in einem Bauer, der rief
sentences.l: I went alone through a wood and came at last to a house; there was not a living soul within, but on the wall was a bird in a cage, which cried
sentences.i: I walked alone through a wood and came at last to a house; there was not a living soul within, but on the wall was a bird in a cage, which called out:
sentences.n: The bride now retells the story point for point; note how her account echoes the narrative above almost word for word.

sentences.t:
„kehr um, kehr um, du junge Braut,
du bist in einem Mörderhaus.“
sentences.l:
“turn back, turn back, you young bride,
you are in a murderers’ house.”
sentences.n: The warning refrain a third time — now on the bride’s own lips, sprung on the guests as part of her ‘dream.’

sentences.t: Und rief es noch einmal.
sentences.l: And it cried it once more.
sentences.i: And it called it out once more.

sentences.t: Mein Schatz, das träumte mir nur.
sentences.l: My treasure, I only dreamed that.
sentences.i: My darling, I only dreamed it.
sentences.n: “Schatz” (literally ‘treasure’) is a term of endearment, ‘darling.’ “das träumte mir” — träumen with the dative person (‘I dreamed that’; literally ‘that dreamed to me’). This soothing refrain lets her tell the truth to the murderer’s face while he thinks it mere fancy.

sentences.t: Da gieng ich durch alle Stuben, und alle waren leer, und es war so unheimlich darin; ich stieg endlich hinab in den Keller, da saß eine steinalte Frau darin, die wackelte mit dem Kopfe.
sentences.l: Then I went through all the rooms, and all were empty, and it was so uncanny within; at last I climbed down into the cellar, where an ancient woman sat, wagging her head.
sentences.i: Then I went through all the rooms, and all were empty, and it was so eerie inside; at last I went down into the cellar, where an ancient woman sat, wagging her head.
sentences.n: “stieg ... hinab” (hinabsteigen, to climb down) is separable. Stuben = rooms, parlours. steinalt = ancient (‘stone-old’).

sentences.t: Ich fragte sie „wohnt mein Bräutigam in diesem Haus?“
sentences.l: I asked her, “does my bridegroom live in this house?”
sentences.i: I asked her, “Does my bridegroom live in this house?”

sentences.t: Sie antwortete „ach, du armes Kind, du bist in eine Mördergrube gerathen, dein Bräutigam wohnt hier, aber er will dich zerhacken und tödten, und will dich dann kochen und essen.“
sentences.l: She answered, “oh, you poor child, you have fallen into a murderers’ den; your bridegroom lives here, but he means to hack you to pieces and kill you, and will then cook you and eat you.”
sentences.i: She answered, “Oh, you poor child, you have fallen into a murderers’ den; your bridegroom lives here, but he means to hack you to pieces and kill you, and then cook you and eat you.”
sentences.n: “gerathen” is the 1857 spelling of geraten (in etwas geraten = to fall/end up in). “tödten” is the 1857 spelling of töten (to kill), with the old -dt-.

sentences.t: Mein Schatz, das träumte mir nur.
sentences.l: My treasure, I only dreamed that.
sentences.i: My darling, I only dreamed it.

sentences.t: Aber die alte Frau versteckte mich hinter ein großes Faß, und kaum war ich da verborgen, so kamen die Räuber heim und schleppten eine Jungfrau mit sich, der gaben sie dreierlei Wein zu trinken, weißen, rothen und gelben, davon zersprang ihr das Herz.
sentences.l: But the old woman hid me behind a great barrel, and scarcely was I concealed there when the robbers came home and dragged a maiden along with them; to her they gave three kinds of wine to drink, white, red, and yellow, from which her heart burst apart.
sentences.i: But the old woman hid me behind a great barrel, and scarcely was I hidden there when the robbers came home and dragged a maiden along with them; to her they gave three kinds of wine to drink — white, red, and yellow — and from it her heart burst apart.
sentences.n: “versteckte” (verstecken, to hide) and “verborgen” (past participle of verbergen, to conceal) both = hidden. “kamen ... heim” (heimkommen, to come home) is separable. dreierlei = of three kinds.

sentences.t: Mein Schatz, das träumte mir nur.
sentences.l: My treasure, I only dreamed that.
sentences.i: My darling, I only dreamed it.

sentences.t: Darauf zogen sie ihr die feinen Kleider ab, zerhackten ihren schönen Leib auf einem Tisch in Stücke und bestreuten ihn mit Salz.
sentences.l: Thereupon they drew her fine clothes off, hacked her beautiful body to pieces on a table, and strewed it with salt.
sentences.i: Then they stripped off her fine clothes, hacked her beautiful body to pieces on a table, and sprinkled it with salt.
sentences.n: “zogen ... ab” is the separable verb abziehen (to draw/pull off), with the dative ihr. bestreuen = to strew, sprinkle over.

sentences.t: Mein Schatz, das träumte mir nur.
sentences.l: My treasure, I only dreamed that.
sentences.i: My darling, I only dreamed it.

sentences.t: Und einer von den Räubern sah daß an dem Goldfinger noch ein Ring steckte, und weil er schwer abzuziehen war, so nahm er ein Beil und hieb ihn ab, aber der Finger sprang in die Höhe und sprang hinter das große Faß und fiel mir in den Schooß.
sentences.l: And one of the robbers saw that on the ring-finger there still stuck a ring, and because it was hard to draw off, he took a hatchet and hewed it off, but the finger sprang up into the air and sprang behind the great barrel and fell into my lap.
sentences.i: And one of the robbers saw that a ring was still stuck on the ring-finger, and because it was hard to pull off, he took a hatchet and struck it off; but the finger sprang up into the air, leapt behind the great barrel, and fell into my lap.
sentences.n: “Goldfinger” (‘gold-finger’) here = the ring-finger, the one bearing the ring. “abzuziehen” is the zu-infinitive of abziehen. “hieb ... ab” is the separable verb abhauen (to hew/cut off); hieb is the preterite of hauen. Schooß = lap (1857 spelling of Schoß).

sentences.t: Und da ist der Finger mit dem Ring.“
sentences.l: And there is the finger with the ring.”
sentences.i: And here is the finger with the ring.”
sentences.n: The turn of the tale: the ‘dream’ becomes proof. She drops the frame and produces the physical evidence.

sentences.t: Bei diesen Worten zog sie ihn hervor und zeigte ihn den Anwesenden.
sentences.l: With these words she drew it forth and showed it to those present.
sentences.i: With these words she drew it out and showed it to all present.
sentences.n: “zog ... hervor” is the separable verb hervorziehen (to draw forth). “den Anwesenden” = to those present (dative pl. of the substantivized adjective anwesend).

sentences.t: Der Räuber, der bei der Erzählung ganz kreideweiß geworden war, sprang auf und wollte entfliehen, aber die Gäste hielten ihn fest und überlieferten ihn den Gerichten.
sentences.l: The robber, who during the telling had become quite chalk-white, sprang up and wanted to flee, but the guests held him fast and delivered him up to the courts.
sentences.i: The robber, who had turned quite chalk-white during the telling, sprang up and tried to flee, but the guests held him fast and handed him over to the courts.
sentences.n: “Erzählung” = the telling, narration. kreideweiß = chalk-white. “sprang auf” (aufspringen) and “hielten ... fest” (festhalten) are separable. überliefern = to hand over, deliver up; “den Gerichten” = to the courts (dative pl. of Gericht).

sentences.t: Da ward er und seine ganze Bande für ihre Schandthaten gerichtet.
sentences.l: Then he and his whole band were judged for their shameful deeds.
sentences.i: Then he and his whole band were brought to justice for their shameful deeds.
sentences.n: “ward ... gerichtet” — the passive ‘were judged/sentenced’ (ward is the archaic preterite of werden; richten = to judge, bring to justice). Schandthaten (1857 spelling of Schandtaten) = shameful deeds, crimes. Bande = band, gang.
